meta.description: The Stoic virtue system: the unity of virtue thesis, the nature of virtue as knowledge, and the four expressions with their sub-expressions. V1 weighted virtues independently (30/25/25/20). The sources teach that all four are expressions of a single unified excellence of the ruling faculty — you cannot possess one without all.
meta.derivation: DL 7.92-93 and 7.125 define the four virtues and state the unity thesis. Stobaeus Eclogae 2.59-63 preserves Chrysippus's definition of virtue as episteme (secure knowledge). Cicero De Officiis 1.15-18 identifies the four 'sources of the honourable.' Cicero De Finibus 3.22-25 provides formal virtue definitions.
primary_sources: DL Lives 7.92-93, 7.125 — virtue definitions and unity thesis
Stobaeus, Eclogae 2.59-63 — Chrysippus on virtue as episteme
Cicero, De Officiis 1.15-18 — four sources of the honourable
Cicero, De Finibus 3.22-25 — formal virtue definitions

unity_thesis.description: The Stoics held that all four virtues are unified and co-dependent. They are not independent skills that can be developed separately — they are four expressions of a single unified excellence of the ruling faculty.
unity_thesis.source: DL Lives 7.125: 'whoever has one virtue has all, for they are inseparable'; Stobaeus Eclogae 2.63 (citing Chrysippus)
unity_thesis.stobaeus_on_sage: 'They say that the wise person does everything according to all the virtues. For his every action is perfect; therefore he is bereft of none of the virtues.'
unity_thesis.implication_for_scoring: A deficiency in any one domain indicates a deficiency in the whole. The score reflects the quality of the unified disposition, not a weighted average of independent parts.

nature_of_virtue.description: Virtue is not a habit or skill. It is episteme — secure knowledge — applied to the domain of human action. Specifically, it is a stable disposition (hexis) of the ruling faculty to judge correctly about what is good, bad, and indifferent.

key_properties.description: Not opinion (doxa). It cannot be lost once fully acquired.
Not a temporary state but a settled condition of the soul.
Nothing external can be added to make it better.
key_properties.source: Cicero De Finibus 3.4: 'Nothing can be added to excellence to make it better'

key_properties.description: The four expressions are inseparable. Whoever has one has all.

no_intermediate.description: The Stoics held there is nothing intermediate between virtue and vice.
no_intermediate.stobaeus: 'There is nothing intermediate between virtue and vice. For all humans possess impulses from nature toward excellence, and in a certain way possess the account of half-feet according to Cleanthes. Hence, while incomplete they are vicious, but when completed they are worthy of serious pursuit.'

four_expressions.name: Practical Wisdom
four_expressions.domain: What is genuinely good, bad, and indifferent

definitions.DL: Knowledge of what things are to be done, what are not to be done, and what are neither (DL Lives 7.92)
definitions.Stobaeus: Knowledge of what is good, what is bad, and what is neither, belonging to a social creature (Ecl. 2.59)
definitions.Cicero_Off: The virtue concerned with the investigation and discovery of truth (De Officiis 1.15)
definitions.Cicero_Fin: The understanding of what is good and evil (De Finibus 3.22)

four_expressions.opposite_vice: Folly (aphrosyne) — false beliefs about what is good

sub_expressions.name: Good Deliberation
sub_expressions.description: Knowledge of when to act, how to act, and when not to

sub_expressions.name: Good Understanding
sub_expressions.description: Applying general principles to particular situations

sub_expressions.name: Quick-Wittedness
sub_expressions.description: Finding the virtuous path in novel circumstances

sub_expressions.name: Foresight
sub_expressions.description: Anticipating consequences within the limits of what is up to us

four_expressions.cicero_elaboration: Cicero De Officiis 1.15-17 treats this as the virtue most natural to humans: 'All are drawn and led to desire for knowledge and learning.' Two vices to avoid: (1) treating unknown things as known and assenting rashly, (2) giving too much effort to obscure matters while neglecting practical action.

four_expressions.name: Justice
four_expressions.domain: What is owed to others — distributing to each their due

definitions.DL: Knowledge that distributes to each their worth (DL Lives 7.126)
definitions.Stobaeus: Knowledge of distributions — knowing what to give to whom (Ecl. 2.59)
definitions.Cicero_Off: The virtue by which the common bond of humanity is preserved (De Officiis 1.20)
definitions.Cicero_Fin: The fair treatment of others and the proper ordering of human relationships (De Finibus 3.62-68)

four_expressions.opposite_vice: Injustice (adikia) — failing to give others their due

sub_expressions.name: Piety
sub_expressions.description: Right relation to the cosmic rational order

sub_expressions.name: Benevolence
sub_expressions.description: Active goodwill and love of humanity (philanthropia)

sub_expressions.name: Social Participation
sub_expressions.description: Fulfilling roles in community life through oikeiosis

sub_expressions.name: Fair Dealing
sub_expressions.description: Equity — not applying rules when doing so produces injustice

four_expressions.cicero_elaboration: Cicero De Officiis 1.18-20 treats justice as the virtue with widest scope: 'Not for ourselves alone have we been born, and our birth claims a part for our country, a part for our friends.' Foundation of justice is good faith (fides). Two kinds of injustice: inflicting harm, and failing to prevent harm when you could.

four_expressions.name: Courage
four_expressions.domain: What is genuinely fearful and what is not

definitions.DL: Knowledge of what is to be endured, what is to be dared, and what is neither (DL Lives 7.92)
definitions.Stobaeus: The virtue that disposes us to endure what we ought (Ecl. 2.60)
definitions.Cicero_Off: The excellence and unconquerable strength of a lofty soul (De Officiis 1.15)

four_expressions.opposite_vice: Cowardice (deilia) — false beliefs about what is dangerous; or recklessness (thrasytes) — acting from passion rather than knowledge

sub_expressions.name: Endurance
sub_expressions.description: Standing firm against what must be borne

sub_expressions.name: Confidence
sub_expressions.description: Rational assurance grounded in correct knowledge of what is truly threatening

sub_expressions.name: Magnanimity
sub_expressions.description: Not being diminished by external circumstance

sub_expressions.name: Industriousness
sub_expressions.description: Willing effort in service of virtue

four_expressions.name: Temperance / Self-Mastery
four_expressions.domain: What to choose and what to avoid — ordering impulse and desire

definitions.DL: Knowledge that makes impulses steadfast (episteme ametaptotos) (DL Lives 7.92)
definitions.Stobaeus: The virtue that produces orderliness (eutaxia) in the movements of the soul (Ecl. 2.60)
definitions.Cicero_Off: Order and measure in all things done and said, in which propriety (decorum) and self-control are contained (De Officiis 1.15)

four_expressions.opposite_vice: Intemperance (akolasia) — being ruled by passion, craving, or appetite

sub_expressions.name: Orderliness
sub_expressions.description: Right timing and measure in action

sub_expressions.name: Propriety
sub_expressions.description: Acting as befits a rational being in context (decorum / to prepon)
sub_expressions.source: DL 7.92; Stobaeus Ecl. 2.60; Cicero De Officiis 1.93-99

sub_expressions.name: Self-Mastery
sub_expressions.description: Command over impulse and desire — not being driven by the four passions

sub_expressions.name: Modesty / Reverence
sub_expressions.description: The inner check that prevents base action
sub_expressions.source: DL 7.92; Stobaeus Ecl. 2.60; also listed as sub-species of eulabeia in DL 7.116
